title: Write Conventional Commit Messages

problem.statement: You made 3 changes on one feature branch: (1) added a new function to validate subnet masks, (2) fixed a bug where DHCP lease time was parsed as a string instead of an int, (3) updated the README with setup instructions. Name the three Conventional Commits types (type(scope): description format) you'd use for these three changes, as if committing them separately instead of as one big commit.
problem.use_case: Conventional Commits types drive automated changelogs and semantic-version bumps in most CI pipelines — using 'fix' for a bug and 'feat' for new capability isn't just style, it's what tooling like semantic-release reads to decide the next version number.

hints: Conventional Commits types: feat (new feature), fix (bug fix), docs (documentation only), refactor, test, chore, etc.
Keep each message on one line, imperative mood: 'add' not 'added'.
The new function is a feat, the parsing bug is a fix, and the README update is docs.

solution.explanation: 'feat(validation): add subnet mask validation function', 'fix(dhcp): parse lease time as int instead of string', 'docs(readme): add setup instructions' — each commit is scoped to one logical change and labeled with the Conventional Commits type matching its intent, so changelogs stay easy to scan and semantic-release tooling can pick the right version bump.
